sections.heading: The situation
sections.content: Context: The inscription listing screen in SGN (education management system, ~300 schools, thousands of concurrent users) was taking approximately 16 minutes to load for certain units. Users could not work. Scale: 26,705 inscriptions for the affected unit. Discovery method: I used Glowroot (APM) to identify the slow transaction, then examined the queries being generated by Hibernate.

sections.heading: Root cause analysis
sections.content: Two combined problems: 1. Cartesian product in the main query: The listing query joined multiple tables without proper join conditions, causing row multiplication. With 26,705 inscriptions and related entities, the result set exploded exponentially. I confirmed this by checking the actual rows returned versus expected — the database was producing millions of intermediate rows. 2. N+1 for complementary data: After loading the list, the code fetched additional information (student details, enrollment status) one-by-one in a loop. For 26,705 records, this generated approximately 48,000 individual queries. Combined effect: ~48,000 queries + cartesian product = ~16 minutes.

sections.heading: The solution
sections.content:
Three changes: 1. Fixed the join: Corrected the query to eliminate the cartesian product — proper join conditions so each inscription appears exactly once. 2. Server-side pagination: Added LIMIT/OFFSET (LazyDataModel in PrimeFaces) so the screen loads only 20-50 records at a time, not all 26,705. 3. Batch loading: Replaced the N+1 loop with a single batch query (IN clause) to fetch complementary data for the visible page. Result: From ~48,000 queries down to approximately 10. From ~16 minutes to ~2 seconds. 
-- Before: cartesian product returned millions of rows
-- After: correct joins + pagination
SELECT i.*, p.nome, m.situacao
FROM inscricao.inscricao i
JOIN senai.pessoa p ON p.id_pessoa = i.id_pessoa
JOIN matricula.matricula m ON m.id_inscricao = i.id_inscricao
WHERE i.id_unidade = :idUnidade
ORDER BY i.id_inscricao DESC
LIMIT 50 OFFSET 0;

sections.heading: What I can explain in depth
sections.content:
• How I identified the cartesian product using Glowroot trace entries (row count vs expected)
• Why pagination reduced query count (only fetches complementary data for the visible page)
• How I validated functional correctness (compared total counts, spot-checked records)
• Why LIMIT/OFFSET was acceptable here (listing with known ordering, not keyset-sensitive)
• The monitoring I rely on: Glowroot for query count/duration per transaction, Kibana for errors

sections.heading: The problem
sections.content: Context: Same inscription listing in SGN. After the main query loaded inscriptions, the JSF page triggered lazy-loaded relationships for each row to display student name, enrollment status, and course details. Impact: 26,705 inscriptions × ~1.8 additional queries each = approximately 48,000 SQL statements per page load. The database connection was held for the entire rendering cycle.

sections.heading: How I identified it
sections.content: Tools: • Glowroot slow trace → showed the transaction with thousands of query entries • Each entry was a simple SELECT by ID (classic N+1 signature) • The trace clearly showed: 1 listing query + N individual fetches Key insight: The N+1 wasn't just in one relationship — multiple lazy associations were being triggered during JSF rendering (getters called by EL expressions in the XHTML template).

sections.heading: The fix
sections.content: Approach: 1. Added server-side pagination (LazyDataModel) — now only 50 records per page instead of 26,705 2. For the visible page, replaced individual fetches with a batch query using IN clause 3. Populated all needed display fields in the paginated query itself (DTO projection) Result: ~48,000 queries → ~10 queries. Page loads in ~2 seconds. Why not JOIN FETCH? With 26,705 records and multiple collections, JOIN FETCH would still produce a large cartesian product. Pagination + batch loading was the correct pattern for this scale.

sections.heading: Interview defense points
sections.content:
• I can explain why Hibernate generates N+1 (lazy loading triggered outside the planned fetch strategy)
• I can explain why pagination was the primary fix (reducing N from 26,705 to 50 makes batch loading trivial)
• I can explain the JSF-specific aspect: EL getters evaluated during render trigger lazy loads
• I validated correctness by comparing record counts and spot-checking data before/after
• Monitoring: Glowroot shows query count per transaction — I verified the count dropped from ~48k to ~10

sections.heading: My monitoring toolkit
sections.content: Daily tools in SGN production: • Glowroot (APM): transaction latency, slow traces, query breakdown per request, percentiles (p95/p99) • Kibana: log aggregation, error patterns, correlation between errors and deployments • PostgreSQL EXPLAIN ANALYZE: execution plans for problematic queries identified via Glowroot How I use them together: 1. Glowroot identifies the slow transaction and shows which queries dominate 2. Kibana confirms whether users are affected (error logs, timeout patterns) 3. EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the specific query reveals the planner's choices (seq scan, nested loop, etc.)

sections.heading: Diagnosis discipline
sections.content: My approach to any performance issue: 1. Reproduce the observation — confirm the symptom in monitoring (not just a user report) 2. Decompose latency — is it database? External call? Application CPU? GC? 3. Identify the dominant cost — the single biggest contributor (usually 1-2 queries or 1 external call) 4. Fix the root cause — not the symptom (adding cache over a bad query is a band-aid) 5. Verify the fix in monitoring — confirm latency dropped, query count reduced 6. Prevent recurrence — add alerts, document the pattern What I never do: guess, add indexes blindly, increase timeouts as a fix, or blame infrastructure before checking the application.

sections.heading: Architectural knowledge I apply
sections.content: Patterns I enforce in code review (from real production experience): • Never make external calls (REST, LDAP) inside an active database transaction — holds the connection during the entire wait • Always configure connect and read timeouts on external calls — no infinite waits • Paginate any listing that could grow beyond hundreds of records • MDB/JMS consumers must be idempotent — retries will happen • Monitor connection pool utilization, not just HTTP errors These are not theoretical — they come from reviewing and fixing real issues in a system serving 300+ schools with thousands of concurrent users.

sections.heading: The challenge
sections.content: Context: The system needed to generate multiple types of official documents (certificates, contracts, enrollment confirmations) from configurable templates. Each document type had different layouts, fields, and business rules for when they could be issued. Previous state: Hard-coded document generation with copy-paste between types. Adding a new document type required modifying 5+ classes.

sections.heading: The design
sections.content:
Solution — Template Method pattern: 
// Abstract template defines the generation skeleton
public abstract class DocumentGenerator {
    
    public final byte[] generate(Long entityId) {
        validatePermissions(entityId);     // common
        Entity data = loadData(entityId);  // varies by type
        validate(data);                    // varies by type
        Map<String, Object> params = buildParams(data); // varies
        return renderPdf(getTemplatePath(), params); // common
    }
    
    protected abstract Entity loadData(Long id);
    protected abstract void validate(Entity data);
    protected abstract Map<String, Object> buildParams(Entity data);
    protected abstract String getTemplatePath();
}
Result: New document types require only one new class implementing the abstract methods. The common workflow (permission check, PDF rendering, error handling) is shared.

sections.heading: What this demonstrates
sections.content:
• Practical application of Template Method (not just textbook knowledge)
• Open/Closed principle in practice — extending without modifying
• Reducing code duplication across related but distinct operations
• Clear separation between what varies and what stays the same

sections.heading: The situation
sections.content: Context: A colleague proposed solving a data consistency issue by adding real-time synchronous validation calls between two services. I believed this would create tight coupling and latency problems under load.

sections.heading: How I handled it
sections.content: Approach: 1. Acknowledged the problem was real and their concern valid 2. Asked clarifying questions about the expected volume and latency requirements 3. Proposed an alternative: eventual consistency with an event-based approach and a reconciliation job as safety net 4. Created a simple prototype showing both approaches under simulated load 5. The team chose the event-based approach after seeing the latency comparison Interview answer: "I focus on the problem, not the person. I acknowledge the concern, ask what constraints matter most, then show alternatives with concrete evidence. In this case, a quick load test made the trade-offs visible to everyone."

sections.heading: Context
sections.content: Background: After 8 years with Jakarta EE (WildFly, CDI, EJB, JPA, ActiveMQ), I needed to prepare for a role requiring Spring Boot, Kafka, and Angular. What I already had: • Deep understanding of DI, transactions, persistence, messaging fundamentals • Production experience with JMS, which shares conceptual ground with Kafka • Java expertise that transfers directly to Spring

sections.heading: My approach
sections.content: Strategy — bridge, don't restart: 1. Spring: Mapped every Jakarta EE concept to its Spring equivalent (CDI→Spring DI, CMT→@Transactional, JAX-RS→Spring MVC). The mental model transferred; only syntax and conventions changed. 2. Kafka: Started from JMS knowledge, then studied what Kafka does differently (log storage, consumer groups, partitions, replay). Built understanding incrementally. 3. Angular: Applied component architecture thinking from building enterprise UIs. Focused on signals, standalone components, and TypeScript strict mode. Interview answer: "I don't treat framework transitions as starting over. I map concepts I already understand deeply — dependency injection, transaction management, messaging — to the new framework's implementation. The fundamentals are the same; the API surface is what changes."
